skill.name: provider-management
skill.description: Manage AI provider priorities, authentication methods, usage limits, and fallback behavior across common model vendors.
skill.summary: Configure provider priority, authentication, limits, and fallback routing for multi-provider AI workflows.
skill.author: AttendantLion8

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command hits, but review shows these are Markdown inline code spans and fenced examples rather than executable scripts. The real risks are semantic: the skill documents command-line handling of API keys and session tokens, and it stores credentials and OAuth tokens under a hidden home-directory path without verifiable encryption.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Provider Credentials Are Entered Through Commands
medium_findings.description: The documented setup flow passes API keys and subscription session tokens through slash-command arguments. This can expose secrets in chat transcripts, logs, command history, telemetry, or shared screenshots depending on the host environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly shows API keys and session tokens supplied as command arguments. The exposure risk is clear, although the actual host logging behavior is not implemented in this skill file.

medium_findings.title: Local Credential And OAuth Token Storage Requires Stronger Guarantees
medium_findings.description: The skill documents auth.json and tokens.json under a hidden home-directory path and mentions file permissions, but no implementation is present to verify encryption, permission enforcement, token rotation, or safe deletion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented storage of credentials and OAuth tokens is explicit. The severity is moderated because this repository shard contains documentation only and no code that writes the files.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick detections come from Markdown inline code spans and fenced bash examples for provider commands, model names, and provider IDs. No standalone script, shell invocation, or dynamic command construction is present in the scanned file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of SKILL.md shows Markdown documentation and command examples, not executable code. The analyzer likely treated Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Token And Model Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker detections are not supported by evidence in the file. The flagged areas contain metadata, auth labels, model names, or credential examples, and no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain weak cryptographic algorithms or crypto operations. The static result appears to be a keyword collision in prose or model/provider names.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detection Is Troubleshooting Guidance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance hit is tied to a troubleshooting sentence about ensuring a session token is valid. No host enumeration, environment discovery, or system information collection is described at the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines only instruct users to replace an expired subscription token. There is no evidence of reconnaissance behavior in the scanned file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command-Line Secret Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflows accept provider API keys and session tokens as visible command arguments. Marketplace users should prefer secret prompts, redaction, and dedicated credential stores.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly show secrets in command arguments. The exact runtime exposure depends on the host application.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Token Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration section lists local files for credentials and OAuth tokens. The skill does not include implementation evidence for encryption, redaction, access checks, or token cleanup.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage paths are documented clearly, but this shard does not include executable storage code to confirm the final handling.

content.value_statement: Teams using many AI providers need predictable routing, limits, and authentication choices. This skill documents provider priority, model mappings, credential setup, usage caps, and fallback behavior.
actual_capabilities: Lists supported model families and provider mappings for Claude, GPT, Gemini, Grok, Llama, DeepSeek, Mistral, Cohere, and Qwen.
Documents authentication setup for subscription tokens, OAuth credentials, and direct API keys.
Explains provider priority ordering by authentication method and manual provider ordering commands.
Describes usage limit configuration and provider status checks.
Shows automatic and manual provider switching workflows for fallback routing.
limitations: The shard contains documentation only; no command implementation is included for review.
Security of credential storage cannot be verified from the available file.
Provider availability, model names, and subscription behavior may change over time.
Users must supply valid provider credentials and follow each provider policy.

use_cases.title: Standardize Provider Routing
use_cases.description: Define which providers should handle each model family and how fallback should occur when a preferred route is unavailable.

use_cases.title: Control Usage Across Vendors
use_cases.description: Set daily and monthly usage limits so teams can reduce surprise spend and preserve capacity for priority workflows.

use_cases.title: Configure Personal Auth Options
use_cases.description: Compare subscription, OAuth, and API key setup paths for common AI providers before choosing a workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Check Provider Status
prompt_templates.prompt: Show my configured provider status and identify which providers are ready to use.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Provider Priority
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set a provider priority order for Claude models using subscription first, API second, and Bedrock as fallback.

prompt_templates.title: Set Usage Limits
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me define conservative daily and monthly limits for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google providers.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Fallback Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my multi-provider fallback plan for reliability, cost control, credential risk, and provider-specific model mapping issues.

output_examples.input: List all configured providers and their readiness.
output_examples.output: A concise status view showing each provider, authentication type, remaining capacity, and whether it is eligible for routing.

output_examples.input: Explain why a request used Bedrock instead of the Anthropic API.
output_examples.output: A routing explanation that compares priority, usage limits, credential status, and provider-specific model availability.

output_examples.input: Recommend a safe setup for Claude and Gemini fallback.
output_examples.output: A provider order with subscription routes first, OAuth or API routes as backups, and conservative usage limits for each vendor.

best_practices: Use secret prompts or a trusted credential store instead of placing keys in visible command text.
Configure at least two providers for important model families before enabling automatic fallback.
Set usage limits below actual quotas so fallback can happen before hard provider failures.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real API keys or session tokens into shared chats, tickets, or screenshots.
Do not rely on one provider route for critical workflows when fallback is available.
Do not assume documented model IDs remain current without checking provider release notes.

faq.question: Does this skill include executable provider code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed shard contains a single SKILL.md file with documentation and command examples.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. The main concerns are credential handling guidance and unverified token storage details.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The static command findings are mostly false positives, but the skill handles API keys, session tokens, and OAuth tokens.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it manage usage limits?
faq.answer: The documentation describes daily and monthly usage limit commands and provider status checks.

faq.question: What should users verify before use?
faq.answer: Users should verify credential storage, redaction behavior, file permissions, and current provider model IDs.
